fields.brief_block: Excessive or unlawful force, even when acting in self-defense, is not justifiable.
fields.opinion_block: [A] person is justified in using force against another when and to the extent that he or she reasonably believes that such threat or force is necessary to defend himself or herself ... a person is justified in using force which is intended or likely to cause death or great bodily harm only if he or she reasonably believes that such force is necessary to prevent death or great bodily injury to himself or herself or a third person or to prevent the commission of a forcible felony.
fields.finding_analysis:
Chambers v. State is a Georgia aggravated-assault appeal in which the defendant's justification defense was at issue. The opinion recites OCGA § 16-3-21 (a), which limits justified force to what is reasonably necessary -- and deadly force to what is necessary to prevent death or great bodily harm -- so the necessity/proportionality framework underlying the brief's proposition is genuinely present in the text.

But the case does not hold that excessive or unlawful force in self-defense is unjustifiable. Its actual rulings are narrower: that the trial court did not abuse its discretion in limiting cross-examination, and that Chambers failed to make even a prima facie showing of justification because there was no evidence the victim was the aggressor. The brief generalizes the recited statutory rule into a stand-alone proposition the court neither decided nor articulated as a corollary, so the citation overstates what the case supports.

fields.brief_block: Hindsight has no place in an assessment of the performance of trial counsel.
fields.opinion_block: Trial counsel also testified that perhaps he should have asked the trial court to question the juror, but "hindsight has no place in an assessment of the performance of trial counsel." Keener v. State, 301 Ga. 848, 850 (2) (804 SE2d 383) (2017) (cleaned up).
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion directly supports the proposition. Clark v. State is a felony-murder appeal that rejects an ineffective-assistance-of-counsel claim, and in its deficient-performance analysis the court states the exact principle the brief cites: "hindsight has no place in an assessment of the performance of trial counsel." The quote is verbatim. The only minor wrinkle is attribution — Clark itself draws the language (cleaned up) from Keener v. State, 301 Ga. 848, 850 (2017) — but Clark adopts and applies the principle, so citing it for this proposition is accurate.

fields.brief_block: [a]lthough the defendant requested a charge on citizen's arrest, the trial court properly charged justification, which covered the substance of his defense. A trial court is not required to give redundant instructions.
fields.opinion_block: Thus, as Durden did not establish an evidentiary foundation to authorize a charge on the defense of accident, the trial court did not err in denying his request.
fields.finding_analysis:
Durden v. State is a criminal appeal from convictions for aggravated assault and aggravated battery, arising from the defendant's sword attack on his wife. Its only jury-instruction holding concerns the affirmative defense of accident: the court held that because Durden never admitted committing the act and offered no evidence supporting accident, the trial court did not err in refusing to charge the jury on that defense. The opinion says nothing about citizen's arrest, about a justification charge covering the substance of a requested defense, or about any rule that a trial court need not give redundant instructions.

The quoted sentence does not appear anywhere in the opinion (consistent with the FABRICATED quote check), and more fundamentally the principle the brief attributes to Durden — that a properly given justification charge excuses the refusal of a redundant citizen's-arrest charge — is absent from the case. Durden's actual jury-charge rule turns on whether there was an evidentiary foundation for the requested instruction, not on redundancy. The citation does not support the proposition.

fields.brief_block: "[t]he testimony of a single witness is generally sufficient to establish a fact."
fields.opinion_block: In Georgia, an individual may not be convicted of a felony solely on the testimony of an accomplice. The accomplice's testimony "must be supported by independent corroborating evidence as to the identity and participation of the accused tending to connect him to the crime [and] leading to the inference that he is guilty."
fields.finding_analysis:
Goldsby v. State touches on witness sufficiency, but only through the accomplice-corroboration exception, not the general single-witness rule the brief invokes. In Section 7 the court holds that an accused "may not be convicted of a felony solely on the testimony of an accomplice" and that such testimony "must be supported by independent corroborating evidence" — the opposite emphasis from the brief's proposition that one witness is generally enough.

The quoted sentence, "the testimony of a single witness is generally sufficient to establish a fact" (the opening clause of OCGA § 24-4-8), does not appear anywhere in the opinion; Goldsby cites § 24-4-8 only for the accomplice exception (footnote 34). The brief thus attributes a general-sufficiency principle to a case that neither states it nor turns on it, and the matched discussion actually cuts the other way.

fields.brief_block: Trial counsel was not deficient for failing to request an unsupported instruction; no ineffective assistance for failing to request a charge not adjusted to the evidence.
fields.opinion_block: Given this evidence, it was error for the trial court to refuse to give the requested instruction.
fields.finding_analysis:
Hamm v. State is a direct appeal of a murder conviction addressing whether the trial court erred in refusing defense counsel's requested jury instruction on accomplice corroboration. It does not address ineffective assistance of counsel, the Strickland deficiency standard, or whether counsel acts deficiently by failing to request a charge not adjusted to the evidence.

The opinion holds the opposite of the proposition's instructional premise: because there was slight evidence that the State's witness was an accomplice, the requested instruction should have been given, and refusing it was error (though harmless here). The case is about the trial court's duty to give a supported, requested charge, not about whether counsel was deficient for declining to request an unsupported one. The brief attributes an ineffective-assistance principle to a case that contains no ineffective-assistance discussion at all.

fields.brief_block: The grant or denial of a motion for new trial is a matter within the sound discretion of the trial court and will not be disturbed if there is any evidence to authorize it.
fields.finding_analysis: Hargrave v. State states this exact rule verbatim at the outset of its Division 2 analysis of the defendant's motion-for-new-trial claim: "The grant or denial of a motion for new trial is a matter within the sound discretion of the trial court and will not be disturbed if there is any evidence to authorize it." The quote is accurate and the pincite (855) is correct. The opinion attributes the language to Taylor v. State, but that is ordinary practice -- the cited case adopts and applies the principle, and the brief's proposition is a faithful statement of what Hargrave holds.

fields.brief_block: The defendant must make an affirmative showing that the witness would have testified and that the testimony would have made a difference.
fields.finding_analysis:
The pinpoint citation "Bryant v. State, 268 Ga. App. 362 (2004)" does not resolve to Bryant v. State. The document at that reporter location is In the Interest of D. L., a Georgia Court of Appeals opinion (Smith, C.J., decided June 15, 2004) affirming the termination of a father's parental rights by the Juvenile Court of DeKalb County; its page labels run 361-365, so page 362 falls squarely within it.

In the Interest of D. L. is a parental-rights-termination case addressing deprivation, reunification plans, substance abuse, and the best interests of the children. It contains nothing about a criminal defendant, ineffective assistance of counsel, or the failure to call a witness, and it nowhere states that a party must make an affirmative showing that a witness would have testified and that the testimony would have made a difference. The proposition the brief attributes to this citation does not appear in the case the citation actually resolves to.

fields.brief_block: The standard for reviewing the sufficiency of evidence is whether the evidence was sufficient for a rational trier of fact to find beyond a reasonable doubt that the defendant was guilty of the charged offenses
fields.opinion_block: [T]he relevant question is whether, after viewing the evidence in the light most favorable to the prosecution, any rational trier of fact could have found the essential elements of the crime beyond a reasonable doubt.
fields.finding_analysis: Jackson v. Virginia is the controlling Supreme Court decision establishing exactly this sufficiency-of-the-evidence standard, and the opinion states it directly at page 319 and again in its holding at page 324 ("the applicant is entitled to habeas corpus relief if it is found that upon the record evidence adduced at the trial no rational trier of fact could have found proof of guilt beyond a reasonable doubt"). The brief's articulation accurately tracks the holding.

fields.brief_block: The terms "in his presence" and "within his immediate knowledge" are synonymous under the citizen's arrest statute, meaning that a crime is considered to be committed in a citizen's presence if they acquire knowledge of its commission through the exercise of any of their senses.
fields.opinion_block: The phrases "in his presence" and "within his immediate knowledge" are synonymous and a crime is committed in one's presence if by the exercise of any of his senses he has knowledge of its commission.
fields.finding_analysis: In its Division 3 discussion of citizen's arrest under Code § 27-211, Johnson v. Jackson states verbatim that the phrases "in his presence" and "within his immediate knowledge" are synonymous and that a crime is committed in one's presence if by the exercise of any of his senses one has knowledge of its commission. The brief's proposition tracks this holding precisely, and the quoted phrases appear word-for-word in the opinion.

fields.brief_block: Trial counsel's decisions relating to strategy and tactics are not judged by hindsight.
fields.opinion_block: Trial counsel's decisions relating to strategy and tactics are not judged by hindsight. See Grissom v. State, 296 Ga. 406 (768 SE2d 494) (2015).
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion supports this proposition verbatim. In Division 4 (on page 567, the cited pincite), the Georgia Supreme Court states word-for-word that "Trial counsel's decisions relating to strategy and tactics are not judged by hindsight," citing Grissom v. State, in the course of rejecting the appellant's ineffective-assistance claim about counsel's failure to call alibi witnesses. The quotation is accurate and the proposition is a faithful statement of the court's holding.

fields.brief_block: [a] citizen's arrest defense is not required when a justification charge sufficiently encompasses the defendant's theory of the case.
fields.finding_analysis:
Lang v. State is a Georgia criminal-appeal case about whether a felon-in-possession charge had to be tried jointly with (rather than severed or bifurcated from) a criminal-gang-activity charge, and about the admission of gang-related evidence. It does not address citizen's arrest, justification defenses, or whether a justification jury charge sufficiently encompasses a defendant's theory of the case.

The quoted sentence appears nowhere in the opinion (quote check: FABRICATED), and the opinion contains no discussion of citizen's arrest or justification instructions. The case does not support the proposition for which it is cited.

fields.brief_block: The fact that defendant and his present counsel disagree with the decision made by trial counsel does not require a finding that defendant's original representation was inadequate.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion supports this proposition verbatim in Division 2(a) on page 204, where the court rejects Mahan's ineffective-assistance claim regarding trial counsel's cross-examination strategy and states, "The fact that defendant and his present counsel disagree with the decision made by trial counsel does not require a finding that defendant's original representation was inadequate." The quote is accurate and the pincite to page 204 is correct.

fields.brief_block: A justification charge does not require a charge on citizen's arrest unless the facts specifically support that additional theory.
fields.finding_analysis:
Manzano v. State is a Georgia Supreme Court murder case about whether the trial court erred in refusing to charge the jury on involuntary manslaughter — with pointing a pistol or reckless conduct as the predicate misdemeanor — as a lesser-included offense. It does not address justification charges or citizen's arrest.

The opinion's only jury-charge discussion concerns lesser-included involuntary-manslaughter offenses. Neither the defense of justification nor the theory of citizen's arrest appears anywhere in the text, so the case cannot support the proposition that a justification charge does not require an accompanying citizen's-arrest charge.

fields.brief_block: A charge on justification is proper where the defendant contends that his actions were legally authorized and necessary to prevent a crime or injury.
fields.finding_analysis:
Manzano v. State is a Georgia Supreme Court murder case about jury charges on involuntary manslaughter as a lesser-included offense, the propriety of cross-examining a defendant about whether other witnesses lied, and the sufficiency of the evidence. It does not address the defense of justification at all.

The brief presents as a verbatim quotation a sentence — "A charge on justification is proper where the defendant contends that his actions were legally authorized and necessary to prevent a crime or injury" — that appears nowhere in the opinion; the word "justification" is never used in the text. The case therefore neither contains the quoted language nor supports the underlying proposition.

fields.brief_block: These statutes known as general grounds afford the Court broad discretion to sit as a thirteenth juror and weigh the evidence on a motion for new trial alleging these general grounds
fields.opinion_block: When properly raised in a timely motion, these grounds for a new trial — commonly known as the "general grounds" — require the trial judge to exercise a "broad discretion to sit as a 'thirteenth juror.'" In exercising that discretion, the trial judge must consider some of the things that [he or she] cannot when assessing the legal sufficiency of the evidence, including any conflicts in the evidence, the credibility of witnesses, and the weight of the evidence.
fields.finding_analysis: Montgomery v. State directly supports this proposition in Division 3, which (quoting Hinton v. State) holds that the general-grounds statutes — OCGA § 5-5-20 and § 5-5-21 — when properly raised in a timely motion for new trial, require the trial judge to exercise a "broad discretion to sit as a 'thirteenth juror'" and to consider conflicts in the evidence, witness credibility, and the weight of the evidence. The brief's phrasing tracks the opinion's substance and the quoted phrase "thirteenth juror" appears verbatim.

fields.brief_block: A citizen's arrest charge is not warranted unless there is some evidence to support each statutory element; no citizen's arrest instruction required where defendant did not personally witness the crime.
fields.opinion_block: Since it was undisputed that appellant intentionally fired the gun at the victim and did not act in conscious disregard of the substantial and unjustifiable risk that his act would cause harm or endanger the safety of the victim, there was no evidence to support a charge on reckless conduct. [...] [T]here was no evidence to support a charge on voluntary manslaughter.
fields.finding_analysis: Nelson v. State is a Georgia Supreme Court felony-murder case about self-defense (justification) and whether trial counsel was ineffective for not seeking jury instructions on involuntary manslaughter, voluntary manslaughter, and reckless conduct. It does not address citizen's arrest or any requirement that a defendant personally witness the crime. The opinion does articulate the general principle that a jury charge on a lesser offense is unwarranted where there is no evidence to support it, which is the same evidentiary rule the brief invokes — but it applies that rule only to reckless conduct and voluntary manslaughter, never to a citizen's-arrest instruction. The brief attributes a citizen's-arrest-specific holding (the statutory-element and personal-witnessing requirements) to a case that contains no such discussion, so the citation does not support the proposition.

fields.brief_block: For felonies, a citizen may also arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion if the offender is escaping or attempting to escape
fields.opinion_block: A private citizen may make an arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion if the offense is a felony and the offender is escaping or trying to escape.
fields.finding_analysis: In its discussion of OCGA § 17-4-60, the opinion recites exactly this principle, quoting Hayes v. State that a private citizen may arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion where the offense is a felony and the offender is escaping or trying to escape. The proposition is an accurate paraphrase of language that appears in the cited opinion.

fields.brief_block: For a citizen's arrest to be valid, the citizen must use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances; deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or to a situation in which it is necessary to prevent a forcible felony
fields.opinion_block: For a citizen's arrest to be valid, the citizen must use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances. . . . Also, . . . deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or to a situation in which it is necessary to prevent a forcible felony.
fields.finding_analysis: This language appears at star page *58 of the opinion, the page cited in the pincite, where the court quotes Hayes v. State on the force limits governing a citizen's arrest. Both halves of the proposition track the opinion's text directly and accurately, and the pincite is correct.

fields.brief_block: Excessive or unlawful force, even when acting in self-defense, is not justifiable
fields.opinion_block: “The use of excessive force or unlawful force while acting in self-defense is not justifiable.”
fields.finding_analysis: Reddick v. State directly states the cited principle when setting out the law governing the use of deadly force in self-defense, quoting Clark v. State, 271 Ga. 27, 29 (1999): "The use of excessive force or unlawful force while acting in self-defense is not justifiable." The brief's proposition is an accurate restatement of that rule, and the opinion applies it in affirming the conviction (holding the shooting was unjustified because the unarmed, retreating victim posed no threat). The claim is squarely supported.

fields.brief_block: The failure to request a jury instruction not supported by the evidence does not constitute deficient performance.
fields.opinion_block: The failure of defense counsel at least to object to an argument of future dangerousness may be found to constitute deficient performance.
fields.finding_analysis:
Reed v. State is a Georgia felony-murder appeal that does reach ineffective assistance of counsel, but only as to two specific Strickland claims: counsel's failure to challenge the felony-murder indictment (Division 4(a)) and counsel's failure to object and move for mistrial over the State's improper closing argument about future dangerousness (Division 4(b)). It does not address counsel's failure to request a jury instruction, let alone a jury instruction unsupported by the evidence.

The brief attributes to Reed a specific deficient-performance principle about unrequested jury instructions that appears nowhere in the opinion. The court's only deficiency analysis concerns failing to object to closing argument, and even there it resolved the claim on the prejudice prong, not by holding the omission non-deficient. Sharing Strickland's general "deficient performance" vocabulary does not make Reed authority for the unrelated jury-instruction proposition asserted here.

fields.brief_block: The trial court did not err plainly or otherwise because the justification charge already covered the legal defense raised[.] [...] plain error requires a clear legal mistake that affects substantial rights and the fairness of proceedings.
fields.opinion_block: Second, the legal error must be clear or obvious, rather than subject to reasonable dispute. Third, the error must have affected the appellant's substantial rights . . . [and] if the above three prongs are satisfied, the [appellate court] has the discretion to remedy the error — discretion which ought to be exercised only if the error 'seriously affect[s] the fairness, integrity or public reputation of judicial proceedings.'
fields.finding_analysis:
Kelly is cited for the plain-error standard, and on that point it supports the brief: at page 33 the Georgia Supreme Court expressly adopts the four-prong federal standard, under which a movant must show a clear or obvious error that affected substantial rights and that seriously affects the fairness, integrity, or public reputation of the proceedings. The brief's compressed articulation — a clear legal mistake affecting substantial rights and the fairness of proceedings — accurately tracks that holding at the cited pincite.

Note that Kelly's own facts concern the omission of an 'inherent dangerousness' instruction in a felony-murder charge, not a justification charge or defense; the brief's reference to a 'justification charge' is its application to its own record, and the citation is properly invoked only for the governing standard, which Kelly states.

fields.brief_block: Georgia courts have made it clear that a valid citizen's arrest must occur immediately and without delay. Harbuck v. State, 288 Ga. 768 (2011).
fields.finding_analysis: The citation 288 Ga. 768 (2011) resolves to State v. Rozier, not Harbuck v. State. State v. Rozier is a quiet title action in which the State of Georgia sought summary judgment establishing ownership of an island in the Altamaha River, decided on the standards for piercing an affirmative defense at summary judgment. The opinion on hand is the wrong document for the cited name, so it cannot be used to verify whether Harbuck v. State supports the brief's proposition, and on its own face the Rozier opinion says nothing about citizen's arrest or any requirement that one occur immediately and without delay.

fields.brief_block: To prevail on a claim of ineffective assistance of counsel, a defendant must satisfy the two-prong test: (1) counsel's performance was deficient, falling below an objective standard of reasonableness, and (2) the deficient performance prejudiced the defense to the extent that there is a reasonable probability the outcome would have been different but for counsel's errors
fields.finding_analysis: Strickland v. Washington is the controlling Supreme Court decision establishing the standard for ineffective-assistance claims, and the proposition states its holding accurately. Section III sets out the two components directly: "First, the defendant must show that counsel's performance was deficient" and "Second, the defendant must show that the deficient performance prejudiced the defense." Section III.A supplies the deficiency standard ("the defendant must show that counsel's representation fell below an objective standard of reasonableness"), and Section III.B supplies the prejudice standard ("The defendant must show that there is a reasonable probability that, but for counsel's unprofessional errors, the result of the proceeding would have been different"). Each element of the brief's formulation maps cleanly onto the opinion's text.

fields.brief_block: A jury charge on a defendant's sole defense, such as citizen's arrest, is only required if there is some evidence to support the charge; "but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense."
fields.opinion_block: The trial court must charge the jury on the defendant's sole defense, even without a written request, if there is some evidence to support the charge.
fields.finding_analysis:
The opinion supports the proposition's substance. The court holds that the trial court must charge the jury on the defendant's sole defense, even without a written request, if there is some evidence to support the charge, and it lists "a person's reasonable conduct in performing a citizen's arrest" among the forms of justification that stand on "the same footing of reason and justice." So both the "some evidence" standard and the citizen's-arrest example are grounded in the opinion.

The defect is the quotation. The brief presents "but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense" as a direct quote, but that phrase appears nowhere in Tarvestad. It is a paraphrase of the court's actual "some evidence to support the charge" standard, dressed up with quotation marks. The legal point is sound, but the manufactured quote should be removed or replaced with the opinion's real language.

fields.brief_block: It is for the jury to determine the credibility of the witnesses and to resolve any conflicts or inconsistencies in the evidence.
fields.finding_analysis: In rejecting Vega's argument that the inmates' testimony was contradicted by the medical examiner, the court states verbatim that "[i]t was for the jury to determine the credibility of the witnesses and to resolve any conflicts or inconsistencies in the evidence," which directly supports the cited proposition.

fields.brief_block: A jury charge on a defendant's sole defense, such as citizen's arrest, is only required if there is some evidence to support the charge; "but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense."
fields.opinion_block: As to Virgil's contention that the trial court erred by refusing to give her requested charge on the defense of accident, we find no basis for a charge on the defense of accident under the facts of this case.
fields.finding_analysis:
Virgil v. State is a DUI appeal in which the defendant argued the trial court wrongly refused to charge the jury on the defense of accident. In Division 2 the court held the trial court did not err in refusing that charge because Virgil "did not claim that any contested element of the offense was done by accident" -- the accident defense did not fit her actual contention that she was never driving or in actual physical control of the car. That holding occupies the same territory as the brief's proposition: a court may decline to charge a defense where there is no proper basis for it under the facts.

But the opinion never states the general rule the brief attributes to it -- that a charge on a defendant's "sole defense" is required only where there is some evidence supporting it -- and it says nothing about citizen's arrest. The quoted phrase "but there must be a legitimate basis for the defense" appears nowhere in the opinion; the nearest language is the court's statement that it found "no basis for a charge on the defense of accident under the facts of this case." The brief generalizes a narrow, fact-specific holding into a broad evidentiary-support rule and backs it with a quotation the case does not contain.

fields.brief_block: The use of force is not justified if the individual was the initial aggressor, unless they withdraw from the encounter and effectively communicate their intent to do so
fields.opinion_block: (3) Was the aggressor or was engaged in combat by agreement unless he withdraws from the encounter and effectively communicates to such other person his intent to do so and the other, notwithstanding, continues or threatens to continue the use of unlawful force.
fields.finding_analysis: Section 5(b) of the opinion supports the proposition directly: the court quotes OCGA § 16-3-21(b)(3) verbatim -- which provides that a person is not justified in using force if he "[w]as the aggressor ... unless he withdraws from the encounter and effectively communicates ... his intent to do so" -- and applies it to hold that Wainwright, having "initiated the assault and robbery" by aggressively approaching the victim at gunpoint, was not entitled to a justification charge. The brief's formulation is an accurate paraphrase of the statutory rule the court applied.
